sections.label: Book 1
sections.original:
EPIGRAMMA IPSIUS

Qui modo Nasonis fueramus quinque libelli , Tres sumus ; hoc illi praetulit auctor opus . Ut iam nulla tibi nos sit legisse voluptas , At levior demptis poena duobus erit .
sections.translation:
Elegy I : By Dryden

For mighty wars I thought to tune my lute , And make my measures to my subject suit . Six feet for ev ' ry verse the muse design ' d , But Cupid laughing , when he saw my mind , From ev ' ry second verse a foot purloin ' d . " Who gave thee , boy , this arbitrary sway , On subjects , not thy own , commands to lay , Who Phoebus only , and his laws obey ? ' Tis more absurd , than if the queen of love Should in Minerva ' s arms to battle move ; Or manly Pallas from that queen should take Her torch , and o ' er the dying lover shake . In fields as well may Cynthia sow the corn , Or Ceres wind in woods the bugle-horn ; As well may Phoebus quit the trembling string , For sword and shield ; and Mars may learn to sing . Already thy dominions are too large ; Be not ambitious of a foreign charge . If thou wilt reign o ' er all , and ev ' ry where , The god of music for his harp may fear . Thus when with soaring wings I seek renown , Thou pluck ' st my pinions , and I flutter down . Could I on such mean thoughts my muse employ , I want a mistress , or a blooming boy ." Thus I complain ' d ; his bow the stripling bent , And chose an arrow fit for his intent . The shaft his purpose fatally pursues ; " Now , poet , there ' s a subject for thy muse ," He said : ( too well , alas , he knows his trade ,) For in my breast a mortal wound he made . Far hence ye proud Hexameters remove , My verse is pac ' d , and tramell ' d into love . With myrtle wreaths my thoughtful brows enclose , While in unequal verse I sing my woes .

sections.label: Book 2
sections.original: Hoc quoque conposui Paelignis natus aquosis , Ille ego nequitiae Naso poeta meae . Hoc quoque iussit Amor — procul hinc , procul este , severae ! Non estis teneris apta theatra modis . Me legat in sponsi facie non frigida virgo , Et rudis ignoto tactus amore puer ; Atque aliquis iuvenum quo nunc ego saucius arcu Agnoscat flammae conscia signa suae , Miratusque diu ' quo ' dicat ' ab indice doctus Conposuit casus iste poeta meos ?' Ausus eram , memini , caelestia dicere bella Centimanumque Gyen — et satis oris erat — Cum male se Tellus ulta est , ingestaque Olympo Ardua devexum Pelion Ossa tulit . In manibus nimbos et cum Iove fulmen habebam , Quod bene pro caelo mitteret ille suo — Clausit amica fores ! ego cum Iove fulmen omisi ; Excidit ingenio Iuppiter ipse meo . Iuppiter , ignoscas ! nil me tua tela iuvabant ; Clausa tuo maius ianua fulmen habet . Blanditias elegosque levis , mea tela , resumpsi ; Mollierunt duras lenia verba fores . Carmina sanguineae deducunt cornua lunae , Et revocant niveos solis euntis equos ; Carmine dissiliunt abruptis faucibus angues , Inque suos fontes versa recurrit aqua . Carminibus cessere fores , insertaque posti , Quamvis robur erat , carmine victa sera est . Quid mihi profuerit velox cantatus Achilles ? Quid pro me Atrides alter et alter agent , Quique tot errando , quot bello , perdidit annos , Raptus et Haemoniis flebilis Hector equis ? At facie tenerae laudata saepe puellae , Ad vatem , pretium carminis , ipsa venit . Magna datur merces ! heroum clara valete Nomina ; non apta est gratia vestra mihi ! Ad mea formosos vultus adhibete , puellae , Carmina , purpureus quae mihi dictat Amor !
sections.translation:
Elegy III : To a Eunuch , who had the keeping of his mistress

How hard ' s my hap , to have my fair consign ' d To one , who is imperfect in his kind ; To one , who ne ' er can have the pow ' r to prove As woman , or as man , the mutual joys of love ! Who practis ' d first on boys the cutting steel , Deserv ' d himself the fatal wound to feel . Couldst thou be capable of Cupid ' s fires , Or the least sensible of love ' s desires , Some pity thou wouldst have on me , and grant Thy aid , for what thou canst not know I want . Ill suits thee now , the warrior ' s lance to wield , To mount the manag ' d horse , or lift the brazen shield : Arms are for men , and not for such as thee , Who shouldst from ev ' ry manly thought be free . No banner shouldst thou , but thy lady ' s bear , And have no other leader but the fair . Much it behoves thee then to strive to gain Her favour , and thou need ' st not strive in vain . Consult her pleasure , and her will obey , To favour that ' s the sure , the ready way : Without it , how unhappy wilt thou be ! Life is without it of no use to thee . Thou ' rt beautiful , and mayst thy prime enjoy , And well thy beauty and thy youth employ . Study to serve thy gentle mistress well , And merit her good graces by thy zeal ; Watch as thou wilt , the trouble thou mayst spare , She ' ll easily deceive thy utmost care . When two fond lovers are agreed to meet , Canst thou their well-concerted plot defeat ? The ways of kindness thou shouldst rather use ; By being civil thou wilt nothing lose ; And when an opportunity is fail , For thy own sake be friendly to our pray ' r . A friend be to thy lady , not a guard , And we , with bounteous hand , thy friendship will reward .

sections.label: Section 3.1.1
sections.original: Stat vetus et multos incaedua silva per annos ;
sections.translation: Unhurt by steel , arose an ancient wood ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.2
sections.original: Credibile est illi numen inesse loco .
sections.translation: A mansion fit for some retiring god ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.3
sections.original: Fons sacer in medio speluncaque pumice pendens ,
sections.translation: With craggy stones a secret grot was hung ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.4
sections.original: Et latere ex omni dulce queruntur aves .
sections.translation: And in the midst a sacred fountain sprung ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.5
sections.original: Hic ego dum spatior tectus nemoralibus umbris —
sections.translation: The courting birds repeating songs of love ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.6
sections.original: Quod mea , quaerebam , Musa moveret opus —
sections.translation: With soft complainings sweetly fill ' d the grove :

sections.label: Section 3.1.7
sections.original: Venit odoratos Elegia nexa capillos ,
sections.translation: Here wand ' ring thoughtful , and intent to choose

sections.label: Section 3.1.8
sections.original: Et , puto , pes illi longior alter erat .
sections.translation: Some theme unsung , to please the busy muse ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.9
sections.original: Forma decens , vestis tenuissima , vultus amantis ,
sections.translation: Fair elegy came on with gentle pace ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.10
sections.original: Et pedibus vitium causa decoris erat .
sections.translation: Unforc ' d her air and easy was her grace .

sections.label: Section 3.1.11
sections.original: Venit et ingenti violenta Tragoedia passu :
sections.translation: Her flaxen hair , in curious tresses wreath ' d ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.12
sections.original: Fronte comae torva , palla iacebat humi ;
sections.translation: Ambrosial sweets and heav ' nly odours breath ' d ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.13
sections.original: Laeva manus sceptrum late regale movebat ,
sections.translation: A simple dress the careless charmer bore ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.14
sections.original: Lydius alta pedum vincla cothurnus erat .
sections.translation: And loving looks , and smiles unartful wore .

sections.label: Section 3.1.15
sections.original: Et prior ' ecquis erit ,' dixit , ' tibi finis amandi ,
sections.translation: Next came the goddess of the tragic scene ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.16
sections.original: O argumenti lente poeta tui ?
sections.translation: With stately tread , and proud majestic mien

sections.label: Section 3.1.17
sections.original: Nequitiam vinosa tuam convivia narrant ,
sections.translation: Her front severe , with hanging curls was drown ' d ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.18
sections.original: Narrant in multas conpita secta vias .
sections.translation: Her length of robe was full , and swept the ground :

sections.label: Section 3.1.19
sections.original: Saepe aliquis digito vatem designat euntem ,
sections.translation: Her hand held out , a regal sceptre grac ' d ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.20
sections.original: Atque ait " hic , hic est , quem ferus urit Amor !"
sections.translation: And Lydian buskins half her legs embrac ' d .

sections.label: Section 3.1.21
sections.original: Fabula , nec sentis , tota iactaris in urbe ,
sections.translation: She first ; " Must love for ever tune thy voice ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.22
sections.original: Dum tua praeterito facta pudore refers .
sections.translation: Fond idle bard , and trifling in thy choice

sections.label: Section 3.1.23
sections.original: Tempus erat , thyrso pulsum graviore moveri ;
sections.translation: Thy wanton songs employ the drunkard ' s tongue ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.24
sections.original: Cessatum satis est — incipe maius opus !
sections.translation: In ev ' ry street thy ribald lays are sung ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.25
sections.original: Materia premis ingenium . cane facta virorum .
sections.translation: The finger marks thee in thy passing by ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.26
sections.original: " haec animo ," dices , " area facta meo est !"
sections.translation: ' Behold , where goes the slave of love ,' they cry .

sections.label: Section 3.1.27
sections.original: Quod tenerae cantent , lusit tua Musa , puellae ,
sections.translation: Thy lewd exploits , thou profligate , are grown

sections.label: Section 3.1.28
sections.original: Primaque per numeros acta iuventa suos .
sections.translation: The public theme , and talk of all the town ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.29
sections.original: Nunc habeam per te Romana Tragoedia nomen !
sections.translation: Whilst unconcern ' d , and lost to sense of shame ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.30
sections.original: Inplebit leges spiritus iste meas .'
sections.translation: Thou still runn ' st on nor mind ' st thy ruin ' d fame .

sections.label: Section 3.1.31
sections.original: Hactenus , et movit pictis innixa cothurnis
sections.translation: Enough thou ' st told the plaints of fond desire ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.32
sections.original: Densum caesarie terque quaterque caput .
sections.translation: Now let a nobler inspiration fire ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.33
sections.original: Altera , si memini , limis subrisit ocellis —
sections.translation: Thy matter cramps thy genius , learn to find

sections.label: Section 3.1.34
sections.original: Fallor , an in dextra myrtea virga fuit ?
sections.translation: A manly subject , and exert thy mind .

sections.label: Section 3.1.35
sections.original: ' Quid gravibus verbis , animosa Tragoedia ,' dixit ,
sections.translation: In songs for girls , fond toys , and idle play ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.36
sections.original: ' Me premis ? an numquam non gravis esse potes ?
sections.translation: Thy muse has wanton ' d all her hours away .

sections.label: Section 3.1.37
sections.original: Inparibus tamen es numeris dignata moveri ;
sections.translation: But youth at length has fill ' d its measure up ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.38
sections.original: In me pugnasti versibus usa meis .
sections.translation: My friend , ' tis time to taste of t ' other cup .

sections.label: Section 3.1.39
sections.original: Non ego contulerim sublimia carmina nostris ;
sections.translation: Now in my service let thy force be shown ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.40
sections.original: Obruit exiguas regia vestra fores .
sections.translation: Assert my honour , and retrieve thy own ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.41
sections.original: Sum levis , et mecum levis est , mea cura , Cupido ;
sections.translation: Thy sprightly fancy , and inventive wit ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.42
sections.original: Non sum materia fortior ipsa mea .
sections.translation: The lofty style of tragic scenes will fit ."

sections.label: Section 3.1.43
sections.original: Rustica sit sine me lascivi mater Amoris ;
sections.translation: She said ; and proudly rising in her gait ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.44
sections.original: Huic ego proveni lena comesque deae .
sections.translation: Thrice shook her tresses , and display ' d her state .

sections.label: Section 3.1.45
sections.original: Quam tu non poteris duro reserare cothurno ,
sections.translation: With open look ( nor was my sight beguil ' d )

sections.label: Section 3.1.46
sections.original: Haec est blanditiis ianua laxa meis ;
sections.translation: And joyous eyes her rival sweetly smil ' d ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.47
sections.original: Et tamen emerui plus quam tu posse , ferendo
sections.translation: Sustain ' d her hand a myrtle branch upright ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.48
sections.original: Multa supercilio non patienda tuo .
sections.translation: Or did my fancy form the charming sight ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.49
sections.original: Per me decepto didicit custode Corinna
sections.translation: " Still so severe , 0 , tragedy ! ( she cried );

sections.label: Section 3.1.50
sections.original: Liminis adstricti sollicitare fidem ,
sections.translation: And canst thou ne ' er forego thy sullen pride ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.51
sections.original: Delabique toro tunica velata soluta
sections.translation: I not compare my lowly lays to thine ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.52
sections.original: Atque inpercussos nocte movere pedes .
sections.translation: Too weak materials for the vast design .

sections.label: Section 3.1.53
sections.original: A quotiens foribus duris infixa pependi ,
sections.translation: The style unlabour ' d , negligent the dress ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.54
sections.original: Non verita a populo praetereunte legi !
sections.translation: My verse is humbler , and my matter less .

sections.label: Section 3.1.55
sections.original: Quin ego me memini , dum custos saevus abiret ,
sections.translation: Gay , wanton , soft , my business is to move ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.56
sections.original: Ancillae miseram delituisse sinu .
sections.translation: With melting strains , the playful god of love .

sections.label: Section 3.1.57
sections.original: Quid , cum me munus natali mittis , at illa
sections.translation: Bereft of me , fair Venus wants her charms ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.58
sections.original: Rumpit et adposita barbara mersat aqua ?
sections.translation: I help the goddess , and prepare her arms .

sections.label: Section 3.1.59
sections.original: Prima tuae movi felicia semina mentis ;
sections.translation: My luring art , and soothing lays prevail ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.60
sections.original: Munus habes , quod te iam petit ista , meum .'
sections.translation: Where lofty port , and tragic buskins fail .

sections.label: Section 3.1.61
sections.original: Desierat . coepi : ' per vos utramque rogamus ,
sections.translation: I more deserve , by making that my care ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.62
sections.original: In vacuas aures verba timentis eant .
sections.translation: Thy rigid pride allows not thee to bear :

sections.label: Section 3.1.63
sections.original: Altera me sceptro decoras altoque cothurno ;
sections.translation: By me , Corinna first was taught to try

sections.label: Section 3.1.64
sections.original: Iam nunc contacto magnus in ore sonus .
sections.translation: Tobreak from prison , and deceive the spy ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.65
sections.original: Altera das nostro victurum nomen amori —
sections.translation: I first induc ' d the fearful fair to slide

sections.label: Section 3.1.66
sections.original: Ergo ades et longis versibus adde brevis !
sections.translation: With tremb ' ling caution from her husband ' s side ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.67
sections.original: Exiguum vati concede , Tragoedia , tempus !
sections.translation: When to thy arms , all loose , and disarray ' d ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.68
sections.original: Tu labor aeternus ; quod petit illa , breve est .'
sections.translation: Prepar ' d for pleasure , flew the melting maid .

sections.label: Section 3.1.69
sections.original: Mota dedit veniam — teneri properentur Amores ,
sections.translation: Fix ' d on her door , how oft I ' ve hung on high ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.70
sections.original: Dum vacat ; a tergo grandius urguet opus !
sections.translation: Expos ' d , and patient of each gazing eye !
